metadata.summary: Quantify user return patterns with D1/D7/D30 cohort retention, DAU/MAU ratio, monthly churn, and churn-risk scores. Lock SQL definitions to prevent metric drift.

content.title: Retention Metrics
content.summary: Retention metrics quantify how many users return over time. Core set: D1/D7/D30 cohort retention rates, DAU/MAU engagement ratio, monthly churn rate, and a churn-risk score per user. Every metric requires a locked SQL definition — "DAU" computed differently across reports produces uncomparable numbers.

why.p: Aggregate retention ("D30 = 15%") hides cohort trends and segment regressions. Without cohort-level retention you cannot tell whether the product is improving, whether a traffic-source change is diluting quality, or whether a feature experiment moved the needle. The churn-risk score gives operators time to intervene before users cancel.

list.item: Standing up retention reporting for the first time — generate D1/D7/D30 cohort tables.
Weekly digest automation: WoW deltas, at-risk cohort flags, DAU/MAU trend.
Benchmarking against industry norms (B2B SaaS, social, mobile, ecom) to set realistic targets.
Building a churn-risk model: feature engineering per user from event data.
Pre-A/B-test power planning — knowing baseline cohort variance is required to size experiments.
One-off transactional products (single-purchase utility, gift) — retention is the wrong primitive; use LTV + repeat-purchase.
Pre-PMF: cohorts smaller than 100 users produce noise, not signal.
B2B products with monthly or quarterly usage — D1/D7 retention misleads; use feature-event retention or contract-tied metrics.
Heavily seasonal products where cohort comparison across periods is invalid without de-seasonalizing.

section.name: core-metrics
section.p: Metric Definitions and Benchmarks for D1/D7/D30 retention, DAU/MAU engagement ratios, and monthly churn by product type.

rule.statement: Lock every retention metric definition in a versioned SQL model (dbt or equivalent) before any reporting begins; never let an agent recompute with a slightly different filter mid-week.
rule.rationale: Definitional drift — "DAU" computed as event_count > 0 vs DISTINCT(user_id) — produces numbers that look comparable but are not, breaking trend analysis.

list.item: Dn retention = users from cohort C still active on day N / cohort C size
DAU = distinct users with qualifying event on a given calendar day
WAU = distinct users with qualifying event in a 7-day window
MAU = distinct users with qualifying event in a 28-day window
DAU/MAU ratio = stickiness; measures how often monthly users return daily
Monthly churn = users who cancel or become inactive in month / users at start of month

section.p: Retention Benchmarks by Product Type. Use as reference band (min/typical/max), not a single target:

list.item: Consumer subscription: D1 60-80%, D7 50-65%, D30 40-55%
DAU/MAU poor: <15% | average: 15-25% | good: 25-40% | great: >40%
WAU/MAU poor: <40% | average: 40-60% | good: 60-75% | great: >75%

rule.statement: Suppress DAU/MAU ratio when MAU < 1000 — on tiny products it is noise, not signal.
rule.rationale: A single power user skews the ratio; the metric needs a stable denominator to be meaningful.

list.item: B2B SaaS: good <5%, great <2%
Consumer subscription: good <8%, great <5%
Mobile app: good <10%, great <7%

antipattern.description: Computing DAU from event_count > 0 instead of COUNT(DISTINCT user_id).
antipattern.reason: Multi-session users inflate DAU; the metric becomes non-comparable across time periods.

antipattern.description: Reporting D30 retention for cohorts that are only 10 days old.
antipattern.reason: Diagonal effect: newest cohorts have incomplete history; D30 comparisons are meaningless until the cohort matures to 30+ days.

antipattern.description: Comparing cohort retention across periods with different traffic mixes.
antipattern.reason: A paid-channel expansion confounds the trend; always segment by acquisition source before declaring retention improvement.

section.name: implementation-checklist
section.p: Four-phase checklist for standing up retention measurement: define active user, build cohort tables, segment analysis, dashboarding and predictive scoring.

list.item: determine what counts as "active" (login, key action, purchase) — document the decision
exclude test accounts and internal staff from all retention calculations
calculate D1, D7, D30, D90 retention for each weekly/monthly cohort
verify cohort size — suppress or grey-label cohorts with fewer than 50 users
lock definition in versioned SQL model before building any dashboard
build acquisition cohort retention table (rows = cohort, columns = D1/D7/D30/D90)
add cohort size column next to every retention rate
identify seasonal patterns by comparing same-month cohorts year-over-year
find best and worst performing cohorts and attribute the difference to source or product change
segment by acquisition channel (paid vs organic vs referral)
segment by pricing tier (free vs paid) and device/platform
compare feature-adoption cohorts: users who used feature X vs did not in first week
flag any segment where D30 retention is >20% below the aggregate — this is the intervention priority
create weekly DAU/WAU/MAU table with WoW deltas
set up cohort retention view with benchmark bands overlaid
configure alert if D7 retention for current cohort deviates >20% from rolling median
pair every retention dashboard with a "retention loop" map: which mechanic drives the next session

section.name: churn-risk-scoring
section.p: Three-tier churn risk model (low/medium/high) based on logins, feature breadth, support sentiment, and tenure, with per-tier intervention logic.

list.item: Low risk (score 0-30): daily active, multiple sessions, broad feature use — maintain current experience
Medium risk (score 30-70): weekly active (down from daily), shorter sessions, narrower feature use — re-engagement nudge
High risk (score 70-100): monthly or less active, very short sessions, only checking — personal outreach or save offer

rule.statement: Compute health score as a sum of four capped components: login frequency (max 25), feature breadth (max 25), support sentiment (max 25), tenure (max 25). Retrain monthly after any product change.
rule.rationale: A four-component score is interpretable by humans and auditable; single-signal scores (login only) miss users who log in but never engage with core features.

list.item: s_login = min(25, logins_last_7d * 5)
s_feature = min(25, distinct_feature_types_last_30d * 3)
s_support = coalesce(avg_sentiment_last_60d, 0.5) * 25
s_tenure = min(25, days_since_signup / 30)
health = s_login + s_feature + s_support + s_tenure

rule.statement: Keep the churn-risk agent in draft-only mode — humans must approve before any re-engagement email is sent.
rule.rationale: Automated sends to suppression-listed or long-dormant users hurt sender reputation and may violate GDPR; human approval gate prevents batch mistakes.

rule.statement: Require human approval for cadence before the agent sends re-engagement emails to high-risk users.
rule.rationale: Over-mailing at-risk users causes fatigue and accelerates churn rather than preventing it.

antipattern.description: Churn-risk model trained on Q1 data scoring Q3 cohorts without retraining.
antipattern.reason: Product changes and seasonal patterns shift the feature distributions; a stale model misclassifies users and wastes intervention budget.

antipattern.description: Push-notification spikes from re-engagement campaign counted as "retention improvement".
antipattern.reason: Notification-induced short-term returns must be separated from habitual return — distinguish them by checking D7 retention of reactivated users vs organic baseline.

template.purpose: Retention loop design template: trigger, action, reward, investment, metrics
Weekly engagement dashboard template: retention rates, DAU/MAU, loop health, churn analysis
